snippets.title: Euclidean, Manhattan and Minkowski with scikit-learn
snippets.code:
from sklearn.metrics import pairwise_distances
import numpy as np

X = np.array([[0.0, 0.0], [3.0, 4.0], [1.0, 1.0]])

D_eucl = pairwise_distances(X, metric="euclidean")
D_manh = pairwise_distances(X, metric="manhattan")
D_mink = pairwise_distances(X, metric="minkowski", p=3)

print("Euclidean:\n", D_eucl.round(2))
print("Manhattan:\n", D_manh.round(2))
snippets.explanation: `pairwise_distances` exposes named metrics and Minkowski with tunable `p`. For the point (3,4) from (0,0), Euclidean is 5.0 and Manhattan is 7.0—the ranking of neighbors can change with p.

snippets.title: k-NN with different metrics
snippets.code:
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline

X_train = [[170, 80000], [165, 120000], [180, 45000]]
y_train = [0, 1, 0]
X_test = [[175, 90000]]

pipe_l2 = make_pipeline(
    StandardScaler(),
    KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=1, metric="minkowski", p=2),
)
pipe_l1 = make_pipeline(
    StandardScaler(),
    KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=1, metric="minkowski", p=1),
)

print("L2 label:", pipe_l2.fit(X_train, y_train).predict(X_test))
print("L1 label:", pipe_l1.fit(X_train, y_train).predict(X_test))
snippets.explanation: Scaling before distance is essential when features mix units (height vs income). The same k and k=1 can yield different neighbors—and labels—under L1 vs L2.

snippets.title: Cosine similarity for embedding neighbors
snippets.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

query = np.array([[1.0, 2.0, 0.0]])
candidates = np.array([
    [2.0, 4.0, 0.0],   # same direction, larger scale
    [1.0, 0.0, 2.0],   # different direction
])

sims = cosine_similarity(query, candidates)[0]
print("similarities:", sims.round(3))
print("best index:", sims.argmax())
snippets.explanation: Candidate 0 aligns with the query (similarity 1.0) despite larger magnitude; candidate 1 has a different direction. Cosine picks the semantically aligned neighbor for direction-sensitive tasks.
